Feels like every time arrow functions get involved, things get extra annoying, right? Don't worry — let's figure out what the heck ES6 arrow functions are actually doing.
Before explaining arrow functions, we need to understand the difference between traditional functions and arrow functions. MDN
that an arrow function has no
of its own. The last three aren't the point — the key one is "an arrow function has no
of its own." What does that mean? When you use an arrow function, since it has no
of its own, where does its
come from? It refers to the outer scope — the parent (one level up).
That's why so many of the earlier examples behaved this way:
wandered off at first, but switching to an arrow function fixed it.
